perturbation.original_text: (4) "Product Business" means the acceptance and fulfillment of ---------------- customer orders for Products, including the manufacturing, channel assembly, co-location or centralized image loading and Configuration of Products; provided that the term "Product Business" shall not include on- -------- site Configuration of computer or related <PAGE> hardware or software products made subsequent to the initial image load/configuration.
perturbation.changed_text: (4) "Product Business" means the acceptance and fulfillment of ---------------- customer orders for Products, including the manufacturing, channel assembly, co-location or centralized image loading and Configuration of Products; provided that the term "Product Business" shall include on- -------- site Configuration of computer or related <PAGE> hardware or software products made subsequent to the initial image load/configuration.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Product Business' specifically excludes on-site configuration after the initial image load. The modified definition includes it. This creates a direct In-Text contradiction in Section 3.1(a)(i), where Seller is prohibited from engaging in the Product Business. Under the original definition, on-site configuration would be allowed, but under the modified definition, it is prohibited, creating confusion about the scope of the non-compete clause.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non- ------------------ Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.justification: The original definition of 'Product Business' excludes on-site configuration after the initial image load/configuration, while the changed definition includes it. This creates a direct contradiction with Section 3.1(a)(i), where the Seller is prohibited from engaging in the Product Business during the Non-Competition Period. The original definition allows for on-site configuration during this period, but the modified definition prohibits it, creating a clear and impactful contradiction about the scope of the non-compete clause. This aligns with the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type, where key terms are defined inconsistently.

perturbation.original_text: (3) "Product" means any computer or related hardware and peripherals ------- (including hubs, switches and routers or networking hardware) or software products (including networking software products) which CompuCom has the ability to sell.
perturbation.changed_text: (3) "Product" means any computer or related hardware and peripherals ------- (including hubs, switches and routers or networking hardware) or software products (including networking software products) which CompuCom has the exclusive ability to sell.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Product' refers to items CompuCom 'has the ability to sell'. The changed definition states CompuCom has the 'exclusive ability to sell'. The in-text contradiction arises in Section 3.1(b) where it discusses the procurement of Products from CompuCom by Seller's customers. This perturbation makes the meaning of 'Product' in 3.1(b) ambiguous. If CompuCom doesn't have exclusive rights, Seller may procure from other vendors but with the changed definition, it seems seller can't because CompuCom should have exclusive rights.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will, and will cause its Subsidiaries to use commercially reasonable efforts to cause future Service customers to whom Seller provides Outsourcing and Professional Services and existing Service customers of Seller to whom Seller is able to expand its Services, to procure Products and image load/configuration from CompuCom in accordance with the provisions of Article IV, provided that CompuCom is not competing with Seller to provide future or expanded Services to such customers. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Seller shall be permitted to preserve its ability to resell Products to the extent required by existing customer agreements if (i) the customer refuses to approve of CompuCom providing such Products after Seller uses commercially reasonable efforts to obtain such approval, (ii) CompuCom rejects the customer order for such Products pursuant to Article IV (provided that CompuCom shall comply with the requirements of the Asset Purchase Agreement with respect to such agreements), or (iii) CompuCom fails to meet the requirements of the customer agreement, including but not limited to price, payment terms, return privileges and service level agreements ("SLA's) (subject to applicable cure periods). In addition, Seller shall be permitted (A) to arrange for the procurement of computers or related hardware and software products as agent for customers in connection with future Outsourcing and Professional Services engagements, subject to Seller's obligations under Section 3.1(b) and the conditions provided for in the immediately preceeding sentence (B) to refer orders for Products for delivery outside the -3- <PAGE> United States to comply with Seller's obligations under Seller's international alliance agreements and (C) to perform initial on-site image load/configuration and augmentation in a manner consistent with Seller's current business practices, provided that Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will not actively encourage any Services customer to move its initial image load/configuration business from CompuCom's configuration facilities to the customer`s site.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Product' from simply having the 'ability to sell' to having the 'exclusive ability to sell'. This directly contradicts Section 3.1(b), which allows Seller to procure products from other vendors under certain conditions (customer refusal, CompuCom rejection, or failure to meet requirements). The 'exclusive ability' clause would mean CompuCom should be the sole provider, conflicting with the situations in 3.1(b) where the Seller can procure from other vendors. Therefore, the change introduces a strong in-text contradiction as it limits the seller's ability to procure from other vendors, making Section 3.1(b) ambiguous and potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE IV Acceptance of Product Orders CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Product for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Product is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Product offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Product, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE IV Acceptance of Product Orders CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it may accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Product for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Product is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Product offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Product, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will accept' to 'may accept' in Article IV introduces ambiguity regarding CompuCom's obligation to accept product orders. This creates an in-text contradiction with Section 3.1(b) which states that Seller will cause its customers to procure Products from CompuCom, implying that CompuCom is obligated to accept those orders. Now it is no longer clear because CompuCom 'may accept' the order.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will, and will cause its Subsidiaries to use commercially reasonable efforts to cause future Service customers to whom Seller provides Outsourcing and Professional Services and existing Service customers of Seller to whom Seller is able to expand its Services, to procure Products and image load/configuration from CompuCom in accordance with the provisions of Article IV, provided that CompuCom is not competing with Seller to provide future or expanded Services to such customers. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Seller shall be permitted to preserve its ability to resell Products to the extent required by existing customer agreements if (i) the customer refuses to approve of CompuCom providing such Products after Seller uses commercially reasonable efforts to obtain such approval, (ii) CompuCom rejects the customer order for such Products pursuant to Article IV (provided that CompuCom shall comply with the requirements of the Asset Purchase Agreement with respect to such agreements), or (iii) CompuCom fails to meet the requirements of the customer agreement, including but not limited to price, payment terms, return privileges and service level agreements ("SLA's) (subject to applicable cure periods). In addition, Seller shall be permitted (A) to arrange for the procurement of computers or related hardware and software products as agent for customers in connection with future Outsourcing and Professional Services engagements, subject to Seller's obligations under Section 3.1(b) and the conditions provided for in the immediately preceeding sentence (B) to refer orders for Products for delivery outside the -3- <PAGE> United States to comply with Seller's obligations under Seller's international alliance agreements and (C) to perform initial on-site image load/configuration and augmentation in a manner consistent with Seller's current business practices, provided that Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will not actively encourage any Services customer to move its initial image load/configuration business from CompuCom's configuration facilities to the customer`s site.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'will accept' to 'may accept' in Article IV introduces ambiguity regarding CompuCom's obligation to accept product orders. This directly contradicts Section 3.1(b), which states that Seller will cause its customers to procure Products from CompuCom, implying an obligation for CompuCom to accept these orders. The 'may accept' creates a clear contradiction and uncertainty regarding CompuCom's responsibility, as the two statements cannot both be true without causing logical inconsistencies. According to the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction', the change introduces an ambiguity that leads to in-text contradiction because it makes the obligation unclear.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 Seller's Non-Disclosure Obligations. Seller covenants and agrees ----------------------------------- that, unless otherwise required by law, from and after the Closing: (a) Seller shall cooperate with CompuCom at CompuCom's expense to protect and safeguard all of CompuCom's Confidential Information; and (b) Seller shall not, directly or indirectly, use, sell, license, publish, disclose, or otherwise transfer or make available to others any of CompuCom's Confidential Information. As used in this Agreement, the terms "CompuCom's Confidential Information" means proprietary or confidential information concerning the Business including, without limitation, information regarding prices charged for Products, the assets, liabilities, and financial condition of the Business, names and identities of customers and analyses of the amount and types of Products purchased by each such customer. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Seller shall be permitted to disclose historical financial information, including financial information relating to the Business, as may be required by customers, vendors, lenders or other third parties, provided that such third parties shall agree to preserve the -------- confidentiality of such information.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 Seller's Non-Disclosure Obligations. Seller covenants and agrees ----------------------------------- that, unless otherwise required by law, from and after the Closing: (a) Seller shall cooperate with CompuCom at CompuCom's expense to protect and safeguard some of CompuCom's Confidential Information; and (b) Seller shall not, directly or indirectly, use, sell, license, publish, disclose, or otherwise transfer or make available to others any of CompuCom's Confidential Information. As used in this Agreement, the terms "CompuCom's Confidential Information" means proprietary or confidential information concerning the Business including, without limitation, information regarding prices charged for Products, the assets, liabilities, and financial condition of the Business, names and identities of customers and analyses of the amount and types of Products purchased by each such customer. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Seller shall be permitted to disclose historical financial information, including financial information relating to the Business, as may be required by customers, vendors, lenders or other third parties, provided that such third parties shall agree to preserve the -------- confidentiality of such information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'all of CompuCom's Confidential Information' to 'some of CompuCom's Confidential Information', we introduce an ambiguity about the extent of Seller's obligation. This in-text contradicts with the subsequent definition of "CompuCom's Confidential Information", which lists specific types of information and implies that all such information is covered. The word 'some' creates uncertainty about which parts of the listed information Seller must protect. This contradiction affects Article VI section 6.5 because it's unclear what information is protected and what is not.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.5 Specific Performance. The parties acknowledge that money damages -------------------- are not an adequate remedy for violations of this Agreement and that any party may, in its sole discretion, apply to a court of competent jurisdiction for specific performance or injunctive or such other relief as such court may deem just and proper in order to enforce this Agreement or prevent any violation hereof and, to the extent permitted by applicable law, each party waives any objection to the imposition of such relief.
perturbation.justification: The change from "all of CompuCom's Confidential Information" to "some of CompuCom's Confidential Information" in section 5.1(a) introduces ambiguity. This directly contradicts the definition of 'CompuCom's Confidential Information', which implies a specific and comprehensive list. The ambiguity undermines the enforceability of the contract, as highlighted in section 6.5 regarding specific performance, because it is unclear exactly which information is protected, creating a strong in-text contradiction by weakening the obligation and creating uncertainty in enforcement.

perturbation.original_text: 6.3 Successors and Assigns. This Agreement shall be binding upon and ---------------------- shall inure to the benefit of and be enforceable by the parties and their respective successors and assigns, including without limitation by merger or otherwise. This Agreement shall not be assignable.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.3 Successors and Assigns. This Agreement shall be binding upon and ---------------------- shall inure to the benefit of and be enforceable by the parties and their respective successors and assigns, including without limitation by merger or otherwise. This Agreement shall be assignable with written consent of CompuCom.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement states that the agreement shall not be assignable. The changed definition says it is assignable with written consent of CompuCom. The in-text contradiction here, is if the agreement is binding to all successors or assigns then why restrict it by saying it needs consent of CompuCom to be assigned. This directly affects any legal challenge to enforceability where any successor or assignee is involved.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.3 Successors and Assigns. This Agreement shall be binding upon and ---------------------- shall inure to the benefit of and be enforceable by the parties and their respective successors and assigns, including without limitation by merger or otherwise. This Agreement shall be assignable with written consent of CompuCom.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the agreement is not assignable, while the changed text states it is assignable with written consent of CompuCom. Although the contradicted text is in the same location as the changed text, the change directly contradicts the original language of that section, creating a clear and direct conflict. As the explanation states, this directly affects any legal challenge to enforceability where any successor or assignee is involved.
